subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of aim with explained real-world relevance
Accurate description of selective accessibility model and its connection to the task
Correct identification of IV and DV in hypothesis
weaknesses: Dependent variable not fully operationalized (units/range unspecified)
Minor factual inaccuracy (incorrect year for Tversky et al.)
Relevance examples could more directly tie back to anchoring theory
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (aim/relevance and theory/model) robustly meet the 5â6 band descriptors, with clear explanations; the hypothesis/variables strand sits at the top of the 3â4 band due to partial operationalization. Overall, a holistic 5 reflects strong performance with minor precision issues, consistent with strand assessments.

strengths: Research design is not only described but justified (avoids practice effects)
Purposive sampling technique clearly linked to controlling prior knowledge
Controlled variables and materials are listed with rationale for reducing confounds
weaknesses: Rationale for stress induction via independent-samples design is unsubstantiated
Discrepancy in time limit (5 s vs. 10 s) undermines consistency
Details on seating distance control are insufficient
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall solidly in the 3â4 band: explanations are provided but lack full evidential support or methodological precision. A score of 3 reflects explained design, sampling, and controls with room to tighten justification and consistency.

strengths: Appropriate and accurate application of chi-square test with clear presentation of statistics
Interpretation links p-value and chi-square result back to the anchoring hypothesis
Transparent inferential statistics table (observed vs. expected values)
weaknesses: No effect size reported to complement chi-square
Graphical presentation contains labeling and formatting errors
Descriptive statistics table is empty and fails to summarize data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis and interpretation strands are at the top of the 5â6 band, demonstrating correct, justified inferential work and hypothesis linkage. The graphical presentation strand sits at 3â4 due to significant presentation errors. Holistically, a score of 5 aligns with strong analytic procedure tempered by visualization and reporting omissions.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are stated, explained, and directly tied to study validity
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and are well justified
weaknesses: Discussion of findings is brief and somewhat speculative with limited theoretical depth
Claims (e.g., stress-heuristic link) lack citation and empirical support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While strengths/limitations and modifications meet the 5â6 band descriptors, the findings discussion sits at the 3â4 band due to its brevity and speculative nature. A balanced score of 4 reflects strong critical insight in parts, offset by underdeveloped discussion.

strandSelections.reasoning: Bar charts are used and relate directly to the hypothesis (proportion of âYesâ vs âNoâ in each condition). However, axes, titles and labels are minimal or partially illegible in the submission images; there is no combined graph comparing both groups. These presentation errors place it in the âaddresses hypothesis but contains errorsâ band.

The student clearly interprets the obtained p-value (<.05) to reject the null and explicitly links this to anchoring theory and the stated hypothesis. Descriptive patterns (more âYesâ in ascending) are also noted. Slight over-interpretation about stress prevents awarding the top mark, but the requirements for full band achievement are met.

Both research and null hypotheses are stated, IV and DV are identified correctly, and the IV is operationalised as âequation starting with 1 vs 8â. The DV, however, is only labelled âestimated valueâ without specifying units or range, so operationalisation is partial. This fulfils the 3-4 band; the clarity earns the top of that band.

Purposive sampling is defined and the choice (non-psychology students, English fluency, similar maths level) is linked to control of prior knowledge, showing explanation. Limitations of small, ethnically homogeneous sample are later acknowledged, reinforcing understanding. Minor inconsistencies on mathematics proficiency control prevent full marks within the band.

The selective accessibility model and anchoring are described in appropriate psychological terms, with quotations and lay explanations, and the connection to the present factorial-order task is made explicit: early numbers increase accessibility of congruent estimates. The explanation is coherent and accurate, meeting the highest descriptor. A few wording inaccuracies (e.g., reference year 1979) slightly reduce precision, so a 5 rather than 6 is awarded.

Findings are linked back to Tversky & Kahneman and Mussweiler & Strack, and cultural reading direction is offered as an explanatory point. The discussion, however, is brief and somewhat speculative, lacking depth on underlying cognitive mechanisms, matching the middle band (âdescribed with referenceâ).

The student states a clear aim â to test whether an anchor (starting with 1 or 8) affects 16- to 17-year-oldsâ value estimates â and then gives several concrete reasons why this matters (relevance to everyday decision-making, study skills, time management). The link between the psychological concept and real-life usefulness is spelled out rather than merely named, so the descriptor of âexplainedâ relevance is satisfied. Minor vagueness about how the findings would directly translate into better finance management prevents awarding the top of the band, but the work clearly exceeds the âidentified but not explainedâ level.

Several relevant strengths (modernised procedure, adjusted time limit) and limitations (participant variability, mathematics proficiency, generalisability) are each explained in context, detailing how they affect validity. The explanations are clear and directly tied to the study, fulfilling the highest descriptor though not exhaustively, so 5 points are appropriate.

Materials (papers, timer, projector) and several controlled variables (seating distance, standardised instructions, time limit) are listed with reasons tied to reducing influence and maintaining stress. This moves beyond description to explanation, albeit somewhat anecdotal. Hence top of band 3-4 is justified.

Proposed modifications (broader sample, arrow-direction prompt, alternative question types) are explicitly linked to stated limitations (generalisation, reading direction uncertainty, maths proficiency) and justified in terms of improving validity and reliability, demonstrating full alignment with the 5-6 band; minor brevity merits 5 rather than 6.

The student not only describes but justifies the independent-samples design (to avoid practice effects) and mentions random group allocation, demonstrating explanation rather than mere description. Some methodological justification is thin (stress claim not evidenced), so 3 rather than 4 points are given within the highest band.

Appropriate descriptive statistics (frequencies) and a correct inferential test (chi-square for categorical data) are used; expected values, ÏÂ² statistic, p-value, and decision about the null are presented without calculation errors. The choice of test is justified. Minor absence of effect size keeps the mark at 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The study reference to âTversky et al., 1974â is incorrect (should be 1979), and the connection to selective accessibility could be more explicit by integrating original findings with the current task.
The description of âselective accessibilityâ relies on lay terms; define this concept using precise psychological terminology and cite its core mechanisms to strengthen theoretical grounding.

The relevance section lists benefits (finance, study habits) but lacks direct linkage to anchoring theory; explicitly connect these everyday examples to empirical anchoring effects for stronger justification.
The research hypothesis names the IV and DV but does not operationalize the DV (estimated value) with specific measurement criteria, which complicates subsequent data coding.
Operationalize the dependent variable by defining the exact range or threshold for âestimated value,â ensuring clarity for coding responses and interpretation.
The rationale that an independent samples design induces stress is unsubstantiated; include empirical support or theoretical justification for the assumed stress effect.
Discuss potential sampling bias of using only IB students and explain how this purposive sampling might limit external validity, then suggest mitigation strategies.
Ethical protocols are well outlined, including informed consent and anonymity assurances, demonstrating appropriate attention to participant welfare.
Thereâs a discrepancy between the 10-second timer here and the 5-second limit mentioned elsewhere. Clarify the intended time constraint to ensure methodological consistency.
Specify how seating distance was measured and maintained (e.g., exactly one seat or specific cm apart) to enhance transparency of controlled variables.
The claim linking stress to increased reliance on accessible evidence is presented without source; cite research on stress-induced heuristic use to substantiate this link.
Modernizing procedures with printed sheets and a projected timer is a thoughtful adaptation that increases ecological validity compared to the original blackboard method.
The decision to increase time from 5 to 10 seconds would be more convincing if supported by pilot error-rate comparisons; include such data to justify timing adjustments.
Quantify the impact of participant variability by reporting variance in estimates across electives, providing empirical support for this limitation.
Incorporate a manipulation check (e.g., arrow drawing) to confirm participantsâ reading direction, reducing confounding and strengthening causal claims.
To improve generalizability, propose stratified sampling across various school types (public, private, IB) and justify how this will address current sample specificity.
The future study suggestions (diverse sampling, arrow prompts) are well linked to limitations and demonstrate thoughtful planning for methodological refinement.
The conclusion restates findings but does not discuss theoretical or practical implications; integrate how results advance anchoring theory and inform future applications.
Using a single threshold of 0â9999 vs. 10000+ to code anchoring effect reduces continuous estimates to dichotomous categories, risking loss of data nuance; reconsider this approach.
The chi-square table includes observed, expected, and contribution values, demonstrating accurate and transparent application of inferential statistics.
The descriptive table (Table 1) is empty and does not provide any summary statistics; include means and standard deviations for each condition to support inferential results.
The bar graph lacks axis labels, units, and legend, making it impossible to verify how data correspond to hypotheses; ensure full annotation of visual elements.
How was the rule applied that responses over 9999 in the descending condition count as ânoâ? Clarify this decision criterion to avoid coding errors.
Rephrase âdid not exceed the p-valueâ to âp = .0246 < .05, so reject Hââ for precise and conventional reporting of inferential decisions.
Connect the cultural reading-direction discussion to published studies or cultural psychology theory to move beyond speculation and ground explanations in evidence.
